ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general puri entry (FOODON_03315671) for deep-fried unleavened bread. Bangarpet Masala Puri is a composed dish (puri + spiced white-pea ragda + chutneys); no exact FoodOn entry exists for the full assembled dish. 'closest_parent' match used for the puri component as the most relevant available.
ontology.foodex2_description: Puri — deep-fried wheat bread (FoodEx2 level 3 bread/bakery subcategory)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers deep-fried wheat bread (puri). The full Masala Puri dish with ragda and chutneys is a composite street-food dish not individually coded in FoodEx2; the puri base component is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Masala Puri or Bangarpet Masala Puri as of 2026.

preparation_method: assembled (puri topped with ragda, chutneys, onion, tomato, sev)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (1 plate), comprising 4–6 small deep-fried puris topped with spiced white-pea ragda, tangy chutneys, diced onion, tomato, and crunchy sev.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN (2017 revised edition); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Puri (fried bread) and legume components
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bangarpet Masala Puri as a complete dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for puri (deep-fried wheat bread) and cooked white peas (ragda), combined in approximate proportions consistent with the dish composition (~50% puri, ~35% ragda, ~15% chutneys and garnish). Input values (energy 183 kcal, protein 5 g, fat 10 g, carbohydrate 18.3 g per 100 g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with minor cross-checks against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried bread (NDB 18033) and cooked white peas (NDB 16080). Sodium 300 mg/100 g is plausible given chaat masala and tamarind chutney contributions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — deep-fried wheat bread (puri) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bangarpet Masala Puri as a complete dish. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being deep-fried refined/whole-wheat puri. The University of Sydney GI Database lists deep-fried puri (wheat) at GI ~70 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The ragda (white peas) component has a lower GI (~22–32 per Sydney GI Database for boiled white peas), which partially moderates the overall dish GI; however, puri constitutes the majority of carbohydrate mass, so GI 70 is a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (70 × 54.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 38.4, rounded to 40.7 accounting for chutney sugars.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; estimated 900 mg per standard 300 g serving, approaching 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single dish
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a plate (150 g) and request reduced chaat masala and tamarind chutney, as these condiments are the primary sodium contributors; omitting sev further reduces sodium by an estimated 50–80 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI deep-fried puri (GI ~70) is the dominant carbohydrate source; ragda (white peas, GI ~22–32) and dietary fibre (2.7 g/100 g) partially attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit puri to 1–2 pieces per serving and increase the proportion of ragda to lower the overall glycemic load; requesting minimal tamarind chutney (which contains added sugar) further reduces the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 10 g fat/100 g including 2.7 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat; high sodium (300 mg/100 g) adds cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Bangarpet Masala Puri only occasionally, limit portion to 150 g, and request preparation with minimal oil and no sev to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation; nightshade vegetables (tomato, chilli) may trigger sensitivity in some RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried puri and request a baked or phulka (dry-roasted) flatbread substitute; nightshade components (tomato, chilli) should be monitored for individual symptom response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bangarpet Masala Puri is a popular South Indian street food originating from Bangarpet, a town in the Kolar district of Karnataka, India. The dish consists of small, crisp, deep-fried whole-wheat puris served topped with a thick, spiced white-pea (ragda) gravy, tangy tamarind chutney, fresh green coriander-mint chutney, finely diced raw onion, chopped tomato, and crunchy sev (thin fried chickpea-flour noodles), finished with a generous dusting of chaat masala and red chilli powder. The flavour profile is a characteristic Karnataka chaat combination of spicy, tangy, sweet, and savoury notes simultaneously. Texturally, the dish contrasts the crisp, hollow puri shell with the soft, creamy ragda and the crunch of sev and raw vegetables. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 183 kcal, 5 g protein, 10 g fat (2.7 g saturated), 18.3 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The white-pea ragda contributes plant protein and fibre, which moderates glycemic response relative to the puri alone. However, the deep-fried puri component carries a high glycemic index (~70) and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making the dish a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. A standard serving is one plate (~300 g). The dish is fully vegetarian and vegan, and is commonly consumed as a mid-morning snack, evening street food, or light meal in Karnataka and across South Indian communities in Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bangarpet Masala Puri as a complete dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for puri and cooked white peas, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are broadly consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for deep-fried puri. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from ICMR-NIN and USDA are well-established, and the dish composition is well-documented in Indian food literature.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Bangarpet Masala Puri as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for the full assembled dish are not reported in the consulted approved sources; estimated ~220 mg/100 g from component analogy but not confirmed, so -1 is used per numeric hygiene rules. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite street-food dish are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for the assembled preparation as of 2026. 4. The input record contained a contradictory 'Butter Chicken' ethnic tag; this has been removed as it is inconsistent with the dish identity. Bangarpet Masala Puri is a vegetarian Karnataka street food with no association with Butter Chicken. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): the dish is fully plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero. 6. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; a direct laboratory analysis of the assembled dish would be required for high-confidence classification. 7. The 'country_of_origin: Singapore' field in the input record has been corrected to 'Karnataka, India' as Bangarpet Masala Puri originates from Bangarpet, Karnataka; it is served in Singapore as part of the Indian diaspora food scene.
